visits.text: Allen Cays (also called Leaf Cay) shelter a colony of Bahamian rock iguanas (Cyclura cychlura), a protected endemic species. These massive reptiles, which can reach 60 cm, approach visitors on the beach without fear.
Small sandy coves between Allen Cays' rocks offer calm anchorages and waters of intense turquoise. The total absence of habitation reinforces the feeling of being at the edge of the world.
Highbourne Cay, just to the north, has a marina and small store. It's the last provisioning point before the crossing back to Nassau.

activities.text: Observe endemic iguanas on Allen Cay beach. Do not feed or touch them - species protected by Bahamian law.
Snorkeling on seagrass beds and sandy bottoms around the anchorage. The shallow waters are ideal for observing turtles, rays, and juvenile fish.
Prepare for the return crossing to Nassau: check weather, provision at Highbourne Cay if needed. Plan for a dawn departure tomorrow.

days_plan.itin_title: Island-Hopping the Exuma Cays: From Nassau to the Swimming Pigs

places.name: Big Major Cay Pig Beach Anchorage

visits.text: Thunderball Grotto, west of Staniel Cay, is an underwater cave made famous by two James Bond films (Thunderball in 1965 and Never Say Never Again in 1983). The submerged entrance leads to a cavity lit by natural openings where angelfish, sergeant majors, and snappers swim in shafts of sunlight.

Big Major Cay, nicknamed Pig Beach, is inhabited by a colony of swimming pigs that have become world-famous. They swim out to greet approaching boats. The island is uninhabited by humans - only the pigs reside here.
O'Brien's Cay, nicknamed 'the Aquarium,' offers one of the best snorkeling spots in the Exuma Cays. Dozens of tropical fish species concentrate in its shallow waters protected by the marine park.
The Big Major Cay anchorage, sheltered between islets, bathes in champagne-colored waters. At sunset, the raking light transforms the shallows into a gradient from turquoise to gold.

activities.text: Snorkeling in Thunderball Grotto at low tide slack. Enter through the submerged opening - inside, sunbeams pierce the vault and illuminate schools of fish.
Snorkeling at O'Brien's Cay (the Aquarium). The fish, protected by park regulations, are so numerous and unafraid they surround swimmers.
Observe the swimming pigs of Pig Beach from the dinghy or beach. Do not feed or touch them - respect the wildlife.

days_plan.itin_title: Island-Hopping the Exuma Cays: From Nassau to the Swimming Pigs

map_keywords: Big Major Cay Pig Beach Bahamas

places.name: George Town Exuma

visits.text: Coco Plum Beach stretches along the northeast coast of Great Exuma. At low tide, sandbars emerge for hundreds of meters and the water barely reaches your ankles. The powdery white sand conceals sand dollars waiting to be spotted.
Head to Exuma Point Beach on the northern tip of the island. This expansive beach lined with coconut palms offers a wooden pier and unobstructed views across Elizabeth Harbour.
The small village of Rokers Point overlooks the eastern shore. From the heights, shades of blue unfold from turquoise to cobalt, and the ocean beach below stretches endlessly.
Three Sisters Beach takes its name from three rocks jutting offshore. The sand displays pink undertones and nearby reefs teem with tropical fish.

Walk the loop around Lake Victoria, a two-acre lake in the heart of George Town. The pink-and-white government building, inspired by Nassau's Government House, has watched over the harbor since colonial times.
Catch a water taxi from George Town dock. The fifteen-minute crossing delivers passengers to Stocking Island, a natural 4-mile barrier protecting Elizabeth Harbour.
The lagoon at Chat 'N' Chill welcomes stingrays and starfish in its shallow waters. This sailor's gathering place has been operating since 1998.

Climb to the Monument, a former navigation marker perched on the highest point in the Exuma chain. The 360° view embraces Elizabeth Harbour, the Exuma Sound, and hundreds of boat names spelled out in stones on the ground.
The ocean-side beach unfurls a long ribbon of white sand bordered by low cliffs and dunes. Waves here are stronger than on the sheltered harbor side.
Moriah Harbour Cay National Park covers over 22,000 acres of marine and terrestrial ecosystems. At low tide, a sandbar appears at Stocking Island's southern tip.

Depart by speedboat from Barraterre, north of Great Exuma, for a full-day expedition through the Exuma Cays—a necklace of 365 islands stretching 130 miles. Count on roughly 1 hour 20 minutes to reach the first cays.
Allen's Cay harbors a colony of endemic Bahamian iguanas (Cyclura cychlura), a protected species. These reptiles approach visitors on the beach.

Off Norman's Cay, the fuselage of a Curtiss C-46 aircraft rests in 15 feet of water. The wreck, tied to 1980s drug-trafficking history, is now colonized by corals and fish. About 20 minutes' boat ride from Allen's Cay.
Compass Cay hosts a nurse shark sanctuary. These docile sharks glide around the docks and allow close encounters in crystal-clear water.
Pipe Creek, one of the Caribbean's most stunning sandbars, emerges between two islets. The water is so transparent you can see the bottom several meters down.
Big Major Cay, nicknamed Pig Beach, is home to a colony of swimming pigs that have become world-famous. They swim out to greet boats as soon as they approach.

Thunderball Grotto, west of Staniel Cay, is an underwater cave made famous by two James Bond films (Thunderball in 1965 and Never Say Never Again in 1983). The submerged entrance leads to a cavern lit by natural openings where angelfish, sergeant majors, and snappers swim.
Staniel Cay's dock and tiny village offer a welcome break. Photos from the Thunderball shoot still hang behind the yacht club bar.
Hoopers Bay, just minutes north of George Town, is a sheltered bay with fine sand. Sea turtles still frequent the waters around private docks—approach slowly without feeding or touching them.
Jolly Hall Beach spreads out in a long strip of sand where you're often alone. The shallow, calm water makes it ideal for a peaceful swim.
Rolle Town, on Great Exuma's southern coast, offers panoramas over lagoons of intense hues. The village preserves a few 18th-century Loyalist graves.

Cross the one-lane bridge linking Great Exuma to Little Exuma. Tropic of Cancer Beach (also called Pelican Beach) is the island's longest: a mile-long crescent of white sand traced exactly along the Tropic of Cancer line, painted on the access steps.
The Salt Beacon near Williams Town is an old stone marker from the salt marshes dating to the 19th-century salt industry. It testifies to Little Exuma's economic past.
Forbes Hill Beach, one of the first beaches past the bridge, offers rock formations to explore and typically calm, limpid water.
George Town Fish Fry gathers a handful of colorful shacks about a half-mile west of downtown. In the evenings, music fills the air and platters of fried fish circulate between tables under the trees.
George Town Straw Market offers bags, hats, and jewelry woven from straw or shells, legacy of a Bahamian craft tradition established in the 1960s.
Stroll along Elizabeth Harbour. The natural harbor, protected by Stocking Island, is dotted with sailboats and catamarans. Annual regattas make it the sailing capital of the Bahamas.
Moss Town Beach, between George Town and Rokers Point, is a quiet beach with calm waters, ideal for an unhurried morning with no agenda.

activities.text: Swimming and barefoot walks across Coco Plum Beach's sandbars. Arrive at low tide to fully enjoy the shallow lagoons.
Coastal drive between Exuma Point and Rokers Point via Queen's Highway. Photo stops at promontories to capture the gradient of blues.
Light snorkeling from Three Sisters Beach around the coral reefs close to shore.
Water taxi crossing between George Town and Stocking Island. Boats depart regularly from George Town dock.
Hiking the marked trails of Stocking Island. Paths wind through dry tropical forest, salt marshes, dunes, and hidden coves. Stone cairns mark the route.
Snorkeling at Stocking Island's Blue Hole, identifiable by an orange buoy. Tropical fish congregate here in abundance.
Full-day speedboat excursion through the Exuma Cays. The complete circuit takes approximately 8 hours door-to-door from George Town, including roughly 3 cumulative hours navigating between stops.
Snorkeling at Thunderball Grotto. Enter through the submerged opening at low tide to admire the play of light inside the cave.
Road trip along Queen's Highway between Great Exuma and Little Exuma, with beach stops at every cove that presents itself.
Extended swimming at Tropic of Cancer Beach. The shallow water and gently sloping sandy bottom allow for serene swimming.
Sea turtle watching from Hoopers Bay docks. Keep a respectful distance to avoid startling them.
Rest day with free beach time. Choose between Jolly Hall, Moss Town, or a return to Coco Plum depending on the day's tides.
Late-afternoon walk through George Town: market, harbor, and Fish Fry shacks for a farewell Exuma dinner.

map_keywords: Tropic of Cancer Beach Little Exuma

visits.text: Nassau Straw Market brings together roughly 500 vendors under one roof, steps from Bay Street. Bags, hats, and jewelry woven from straw perpetuate a Bahamian craft once used to make fish traps and carrying baskets.
Prince George Wharf is the cruise ship dock. The waterfront offers a direct view of towering hulls and the capital's port activity.
Bay Street, Nassau's main artery, lines up pastel facades and colonial buildings. The shops' vivid colors recall Georgian architecture inherited from 18th-century American Loyalists.

Arawak Cay, nicknamed the Fish Fry, is an artificial island built in 1969 with sand dredged from Nassau harbor. Its colorful shacks serve conch salad, fried fish, and cracked lobster alongside Kalik beer.

The Queen's Staircase, carved from solid limestone by 600 enslaved people between 1793 and 1794, counts 66 steps (65 visible). It connected Fort Fincastle to central Nassau and was named in honor of Queen Victoria. Waterfalls and dense tropical vegetation now frame the staircase.

Fort Fincastle, built in 1793 by Lord Dunmore atop Bennet's Hill (New Providence's highest point), takes the shape of a ship. Constructed from limestone to defend the port against pirates, it never saw battle and was later converted into a lighthouse.
Cross the bridge linking Nassau to Paradise Island. The panoramic view from the walkway embraces Nassau Harbour, sailboats at anchor, and the skyline of grand hotels.
Versailles Gardens unfold in symmetrical terraces punctuated by fountains and statues—an unexpected calm in the middle of the resort island. The gardens are freely accessible.
Cabbage Beach extends for 2 miles along Paradise Island's north shore. Bordered by dunes and casuarina trees, it's one of New Providence's finest beaches. Walk west to quickly escape the crowds.
Marina Village, at the foot of Atlantis, lines up duty-free shops and restaurant terraces around a yacht harbor. Entry to the commercial zone is free.
Under the Paradise Island bridge, Potter's Cay is Nassau's fish market. Fishermen unload conch and fresh fish, and stalls prepare conch salads to order.

Fort Charlotte, built between 1787 and 1790 by Lord Dunmore, is the Bahamas' largest fort. Perched 80 feet above sea level, it overlooks the western entrance to Nassau Harbour. Its dry moat and underground tunnels are freely accessible.
Cable Beach unfurls 3 miles of white sand west of downtown. Named after the undersea telegraph cable that arrived here in 1892, this beach is New Providence's longest and most popular.
Junkanoo Beach, between downtown and Arawak Cay, pulses with the rhythm of beach bars and volleyball. It's the closest beach to the cruise port, reachable in 15 minutes on foot.
Saunders Beach, just east of Cable Beach, is quieter and less crowded than its neighbors. Locals come to picnic here on weekends.
Western Esplanade Beach, an urban beach between downtown and Arawak Cay, allows a final swim in turquoise waters before departure.

activities.text: Walking tour through Nassau's historic center: from the Straw Market to Fort Fincastle via Bay Street and Parliament Square.
Tasting conch salad and cracked conch at Arawak Cay stalls. Sunday evenings, locals gather in numbers at the Fish Fry.
Swimming and lounging on Cabbage Beach. Rent a beach chair or spread your towel toward the quieter western end.
Walking tour of Paradise Island: gardens, beach, and Marina Village, without needing entry tickets to the resort complexes.
Half-day at Cable Beach. Enjoy the fine sand and water sports offered on site.
Visit Fort Charlotte in the morning, before the heat. Admission is free and the panoramic view over the harbor and Paradise Island is worth the trip.
Final swim at Western Esplanade or Junkanoo Beach late in the day, just steps from downtown.

visits.text: Bay Street lines Nassau's main waterfront strip with pastel-painted colonial facades and duty-free shops. Just inland, Parliament Square showcases three pink Georgian buildings that have housed the Bahamian government since the 19th century.

The Queen's Staircase consists of 66 steps hewn from solid limestone by 600 enslaved laborers between 1793 and 1794. Waterfalls and dense tropical vegetation frame this historic passage that climbs to Fort Fincastle.

Fort Fincastle, built in 1793 by Lord Dunmore atop Bennet's Hill, was designed in the shape of a paddle-wheel steamer. From New Providence's highest point, panoramic views sweep across the harbor and Paradise Island.
Cross the bridge to Paradise Island for sweeping harbor views from the pedestrian walkway. Below, Potter's Cay hosts the fish market where vendors prepare conch salad to order right at their stalls.
Prince George Wharf, the cruise ship terminal, offers front-row views of massive vessels and the constant pulse of the capital's waterfront.
Cabbage Beach stretches for 3 km along the northern shore of Paradise Island. Bordered by dunes and casuarina trees, it's one of New Providence's most beautiful beaches.
The Nassau Straw Market gathers around 500 vendors. Straw bags, hats, and jewelry perpetuate a craft that once served to make fish traps and transport baskets.
Arawak Cay, nicknamed the Fish Fry, is an artificial island built in 1969 with sand dredged from the harbor. Its colorful shacks serve conch salad, fried fish, and cracked lobster.
Western Esplanade Beach, an urban beach between downtown and Arawak Cay, allows one last swim before leaving the Bahamas.

activities.text: Walking tour through Nassau's historic center: from the Straw Market to Fort Fincastle via Parliament Square.
Pre-passage preparation for the Exuma Cays: check weather windows, verify tides, and finalize the route to Shroud Cay (~50 nautical miles).
Swimming and relaxation on Cabbage Beach. Walking west takes you away from the crowds at the resort complexes.
Sample conch salad and cracked conch at Arawak Cay stalls for a final Bahamian meal.

days_plan.itin_title: Island-Hopping the Exuma Cays: From Nassau to the Swimming Pigs
Island-Hopping the Exuma Cays: From Nassau to the Swimming Pigs

visits.text: Norman's Cay remains infamous as the base of operations for a drug cartel in the 1980s. The island was later purchased and transformed into a private paradise, then opened to cruisers. Its sheltered lagoon makes it a choice anchorage.

The wreck of a Curtiss C-46 aircraft rests in 5 meters of crystal-clear water south of the airstrip. The fuselage, colonized by corals and sponges, has become an artificial reef teeming with tropical fish.
The northern beaches of Norman's Cay stretch in ribbons of white sand bordered by coconut palms. Sandbars exposed at low tide create natural lagoons ideal for swimming.
The old airstrip crosses the island from north to south. Now overgrown with vegetation, it remains a visible landmark for navigators.

activities.text: Snorkeling around the plane wreck. The water is shallow enough to observe the fuselage from the surface. Parrotfish, juvenile barracudas, and rays frequent the site.
Beachcombing on sandbars exposed at low tide. Water colors shift from jade green to deep blue depending on depth.

days_plan.itin_title: Island-Hopping the Exuma Cays: From Nassau to the Swimming Pigs

places.name: Shroud Cay

visits.text: Shroud Cay is an archipelago of small islets linked by a labyrinth of mangrove creeks at the northern entrance to Exuma Cays Land and Sea Park. The uninhabited island operates under strict 'take nothing, leave nothing' protection.

Sanctuary Creek cuts through Shroud Cay from west to east through dense mangroves. On mid-rising tide, the gentle current carries you toward the Exuma Sound exit. Green turtles, stingrays, and small nurse sharks cruise these crystal-clear channels.
At Sanctuary Creek's ocean outlet, the 'Washing Machine' creates a natural rapid where tidal current squeezes between rocks. The fine-sand beach beyond is sheltered by a crescent sandbar.

Climb the trail to Camp Driftwood, an observation post built in the 1960s. From this vantage point, 360° views encompass mangrove channels, sandbars, and the Exuma Sound. On clear days, Norman's Cay's airstrip is visible to the south.

activities.text: Drift through Sanctuary Creek by dinghy, kayak, or paddleboard on mid-rising tide. The current naturally carries you toward the ocean beach - time your return before the tide turns.
Swim in the Washing Machine's natural rapid at the creek outlet. The current pushes you toward a shallow sandbar - it feels like a wild water slide carved by nature.
Snorkeling on seagrass beds and sandy bottoms around the anchorage. Starfish, conchs, and juvenile fish hide in the underwater meadows.

days_plan.itin_title: Island-Hopping the Exuma Cays: From Nassau to the Swimming Pigs

map_keywords: Shroud Cay Exuma Land Sea Park

visits.text: Compass Cay shelters a sanctuary of nurse sharks in its private marina. These docile sharks, accustomed to humans over generations, glide around the docks and allow close approaches in crystal-clear water.
Crescent Beach, on Compass Cay's eastern shore, curves in a long arc of pristine sand facing the Exuma Sound. A trail crosses the island from the marina to reach it.
Pipe Creek reveals one of the Caribbean's most beautiful sandbars between two islets. The water is so transparent that the sandy bottom appears within reach even in two meters of depth.

Staniel Cay, a small fishing village with an airstrip and yacht club, serves as the logistical hub of the Exuma Cays. It's one of the few cays where you'll find fuel, fresh water, and a grocery store.
Rocky Dundas, two underwater caves accessible by dinghy from Staniel Cay, shelter submerged stalactites and stalagmites. Sunlight penetrates through natural openings and illuminates the formations.

activities.text: Swim with the nurse sharks at Compass Cay. Walk down the dock steps and let them approach - they've been accustomed to visitors for decades.
Dinghy exploration of channels between Pipe Cay, Compass Cay, and surrounding islets. Stop for swimming on emerging sandbars.
Snorkeling in the caves of Rocky Dundas. Swim through the submerged entrance to admire limestone formations illuminated by natural light.

days_plan.itin_title: Island-Hopping the Exuma Cays: From Nassau to the Swimming Pigs

visits.text: Warderick Wells is the heart of Exuma Cays Land and Sea Park, established in 1958 as the world's first marine park. The northern anchorage forms a horseshoe of turquoise water bordered by beaches and sandbars that emerge at low tide.
The park headquarters on the main beach displays the skeleton of a beached sperm whale. Interpretive panels along the trails explain the island's flora, fauna, and limestone geology.

Boo Boo Hill, the highest point in the Exuma Cays, offers spectacular views over the Exuma Sound on one side and the turquoise shallows of the Bahama Bank on the other. Tradition holds that every sailor should leave a piece of driftwood carved with their boat's name. Legend says the hill is haunted by souls from a shipwreck - on full moon nights, hymns supposedly carry on the wind.
The northern beaches of Warderick Wells, accessible by marked trails, are lined with limestone formations pierced by blowholes. When Atlantic swells run strong, water erupts in natural geysers.

activities.text: Hike the network of marked trails on Warderick Wells. The complete circuit passes Boo Boo Hill, the blowholes, the ruins of Davis Plantation, and the eastern beaches.
Drift snorkeling over coral heads in the north mooring field. Tidal current gently carries you over the corals - nurse sharks, turtles, and spotted eagle rays regularly pass through.
Free kayaks provided by the park - explore secluded coves and beaches around the mooring field at sunset.

days_plan.itin_title: Island-Hopping the Exuma Cays: From Nassau to the Swimming Pigs
